philosophical_grounding: W.E.B. Du Bois — The Philadelphia Negro (1899): primary-source, self-authored, irrefutable.
James Baldwin — Unflinching witness. The numbers say what they say.
Frances Cress Welsing — Systems, not symptoms. Show the chain.
mandate: The largest unified empirical dataset that speaks to the plight of Black American people quantitatively in the way of Du Bois, James Baldwin, and Frances Cress Welsing.

anchors.magnitude: Freedmen's Bureau Special Field Orders No. 15 — rescinded by Andrew Johnson
35+ blocks destroyed, 300 killed, 10,000 displaced, ~$30M property destroyed (2024 dollars: $450M)
4.0M Black Americans migrated North and West
MLK, Malcolm X, Black Panthers, NAACP — all surveilled, infiltrated, and in several cases facilitated assassinations
Black defendants received avg. 40% longer sentences than white defendants for same drug offenses
Black farmers lost ~12M acres — from 14M to 2.3M acres (−83%)
Reduced crack/powder disparity from 100:1 to 18:1 — applied retroactively to 12,000+ cases

pillar_7_environmental_food_justice.philosophical_anchor: Dr. Frances Cress Welsing — analyze systems, not symptoms. The structure beneath the disparity.
pillar_7_environmental_food_justice.key_finding: Black communities face a compound environmental assault: disproportionate toxic exposure, degraded air and water, food deserts, and climate vulnerability — all produced and maintained by policy, not chance.

env_burden_score.label: Environmental Burden — Disproportionate Exposure of Black Census Tracts
env_burden_score.source: EPA EJScreen v2.3 + CDC Environmental Justice Index 2022
env_burden_score.finding: Black communities score in the 66th–75th percentile nationally on composite environmental burden. In Southern Black Belt states, tracts that are majority-Black score in the 80th–90th percentile for pollution burden — meaning they bear more environmental harm than 80–90% of all other U.S. communities.
env_burden_score.key_ratio: Black census tracts: avg EJScreen percentile = 71st vs. white tracts avg = 38th
env_burden_score.mechanism: Urban industrial zoning, highway routing, and waste facility siting — all decisions made by governments that systematically devalued Black life and property.

toxic_facility_proximity.label: Toxic Release Inventory — Facility Proximity by Race
toxic_facility_proximity.source: PNAS 2018 (Mikati et al.) + EPA TRI + UCC 'Toxic Wastes and Race in the United States' (1987, updated 2007)

data.finding: Communities of color make up 25% of population in areas with commercial hazardous waste facilities, vs. proportional representation of 12%
Race remains the strongest predictor of living near hazardous waste facilities — stronger than income
Black Americans bear 1.54x the pollution burden of the overall population; contribute 10% less consumption but bear 56% more air pollution exposure (PNAS)
Older Black Americans 65+ are 3x more likely to die from PM2.5 fine particle pollution than older white Americans (Mothers and Others for Clean Air)

toxic_facility_proximity.key_stat: 1.54x — Black pollution burden ratio vs. national average (PNAS 2018)
toxic_facility_proximity.mechanism: NIMBY (Not In My Backyard) politics consistently placed unwanted facilities in Black neighborhoods because Black communities had less political power to resist — a direct function of voter suppression and disinvestment.

pm25_air_quality.label: PM2.5 Fine Particulate Matter — Racial Exposure Disparity
pm25_air_quality.source: EPA Science Matters (2021) | Environmental Health Perspectives | JAMA Network Open 2024

data.finding: People of color breathe significantly more particulate air pollution on average, across all income levels and regions — EPA confirmed

data.description: Black Americans exposed to 10.4% more PM2.5 than they produce in economic activity

data.finding: Black communities face highest short-term PM2.5 disparities nationally (Environmental Health journal)
Racial monitoring disparities exist for all criteria pollutants; sulfur dioxide monitoring gaps most severe in Black communities (JAMA Network Open)

pm25_air_quality.key_stat: Black Americans: +10.4% PM2.5 exposure vs. their share of economic activity
pm25_air_quality.mechanism: Industrial facilities, highways, and power plants built in and through Black neighborhoods. The air they breathe is not an accident — it is the physical residue of decisions made across a century.

lead_exposure_children.label: Blood Lead Levels — Black Children
lead_exposure_children.source: CDC NHANES (1976–2016) | CDC MMWR 2016 | NHANES Biomonitoring Data

data.finding: Black children in poverty had 4x higher odds of elevated blood lead level vs. white children not in poverty (NHANES)

lead_exposure_children.key_stat: 1991–2004: Black children 4x more likely than white children to have elevated blood lead levels
lead_exposure_children.mechanism: Lead paint concentration in older housing stock — itself a product of redlining that locked Black families into deteriorated pre-WWII housing for generations. Flint, MI was not an anomaly. It was a pattern.

food_desert_access.label: Food Desert Access — LILA Tracts and Black Population
food_desert_access.source: USDA Food Access Research Atlas (FARA) 2019 | ERS State-Level Estimates

data.finding: Black Americans disproportionately represented in LILA tracts — particularly in urban cores and Deep South counties
Black-majority census tracts in the urban South have food store access distances 2–3x greater than comparable white-majority tracts
Grocery store density in predominantly Black zip codes is significantly lower than in white zip codes of equivalent income (USDA ERS)

food_desert_access.key_stat: Black Americans face structural food apartheid — not food deserts as natural phenomena, but the deliberate withdrawal of capital and commerce from Black communities.
food_desert_access.mechanism: Supermarket redlining: from the 1960s onward, grocery chains explicitly avoided opening in Black neighborhoods. When urban renewal destroyed Black business districts, the food infrastructure was never rebuilt.

black_farmer_dispossession.label: Black Farmer Count and Land Dispossession — 1920 to 2017
black_farmer_dispossession.source: USDA NASS Census of Agriculture | Reuters (2022) | Land Trust Alliance | Oakland Institute

data.note: Peak. Black farmers = 14% of all U.S. farmers
1992–2002: 94% of Black farmers lost part or all farmland, 3x rate of white farmers

black_farmer_dispossession.key_stat: Black farmers fell from 949,889 (1920) to 45,508 (2017). Land fell from 41.4M acres to 2.9M acres — a loss of 93%.
black_farmer_dispossession.mechanism: USDA discriminatory loan denials (confirmed by class action Pigford v. Glickman 1999), heir property vulnerability, tax sales, violence, and economic coercion. The Pigford settlement acknowledged USDA's role explicitly.

water_quality_violations.label: Drinking Water Quality Violations — Race and Income Disparity
water_quality_violations.source: EPA SDWIS | AWWA 2021 | Gallup 2023 | ScienceDirect PFAS Study 2024

data.finding: 1982–2015: 3–10% of drinking water systems had health-based violations annually — affecting 9 to 45 million Americans
Over 1 million Black, Indigenous, and other people of color had insecure access to clean drinking water (2013–2017)
Communities of color and low-income communities bear disproportionate drinking water quality violations — AWWA confirms
Race and socioeconomic status are independent predictors of water quality violations — Black communities face higher risk even controlling for income

water_quality_violations.key_stat: 76% of Black adults worry 'a great deal' about polluted drinking water, vs. 48% of white adults (Gallup 2023). This is not irrational fear. It is documented, lived reality.
water_quality_violations.mechanism: Aging infrastructure disproportionately concentrated in communities that were redlined and disinvested. When Flint, MI switched water sources to save money, the decision was made about a city that was 57% Black. That was the calculation.

superfund_proximity.label: Superfund Site Proximity — Racial Overrepresentation
superfund_proximity.source: EPA Superfund FY2022 Population Estimates | Nature Communications 2025 | PMC 2024

data.finding: Black and Asian populations are disproportionately overrepresented in Superfund host communities nationally (Nature Communications 2025)
Racial disparity in Superfund proximity compounds with slower cleanup timelines for majority-minority communities — documented by multiple EPA studies

superfund_proximity.key_stat: In South Carolina: Black residents are 29.5% of the state's population but 55.9% of residents in Superfund host census tracts.
superfund_proximity.mechanism: Industrial site selection historically chose Black neighborhoods because land was cheaper (itself a product of redlining) and because Black communities lacked political leverage to resist.

climate_vulnerability.label: Climate Vulnerability — Black Communities (CDC/ATSDR SVI 2020)
climate_vulnerability.source: CDC/ATSDR Social Vulnerability Index 2020 | Minority Health SVI 2020 | PMC 2021

data.finding: Black/African American women in the United States are more likely to live in neighborhoods with higher social vulnerability than other racial/ethnic groups (PMC 2021)

CDC Minority Health SVI shows Black communities disproportionately concentrated in high-vulnerability census tracts across all four SVI themes: socioeconomic status, household composition, minority status, and housing/transportation
Majority-Black counties in the Gulf Coast face compounded climate risk: flooding, heat, industrial pollution, and inadequate emergency response infrastructure

Hurricane Katrina 2005 — Black residents constituted the majority of those who could not evacuate, the majority of deaths, and the majority of those who never returned. SVI scores predicted this outcome.

climate_vulnerability.key_stat: Black communities are concentrated in high-SVI census tracts — meaning they face climate shocks with less capacity to absorb them: less savings, worse housing, less access to cars, and less political voice to demand infrastructure.
climate_vulnerability.mechanism: Climate vulnerability is the accumulation of every other disparity. It is not a separate problem. It is the compound interest on centuries of structural exclusion.

grocery_access.label: Grocery Store Access — Black Zip Codes vs. White Zip Codes
grocery_access.source: USDA FARA 2019 + Census ACS | Urban Institute | NAACP LDF Water/Food Justice Report

data.finding: Grocery store density in predominantly Black zip codes is significantly lower than in white zip codes of comparable income (USDA ERS)
Black residents in urban LILA tracts are more likely to rely on convenience stores and dollar stores for food, which carry fewer fresh items at higher per-unit prices
2015: 12.8% of U.S. population in LILA tracts — Black Americans overrepresented in these tracts, particularly in urban South and Midwest
NAACP LDF: Black communities face compounding food and water access inequities that reinforce each other at the community level

grocery_access.key_stat: The 'food desert' framing understates the problem. What Black communities face is food apartheid — the systematic, policy-driven removal of food infrastructure from Black spaces.
grocery_access.mechanism: Supermarket redlining, urban renewal, highway construction that destroyed Black commercial districts, and decades of economic disinvestment. The term 'food desert' makes it sound natural. It is not.

state_level_resolution.methodology: Census ACS 2022 | BLS LAUS 2023 | BJS State Imprisonment 2022 | Census CPS 2022 | CDC WONDER 2021 | NAEP 2024 | Census CPS Voting 2022

MS.name: Mississippi
MS.key_note: Humphreys County — highest FDI in nation. 72% Black. 37% poverty. No hospital. FDI + BDI compound point.

AL.name: Alabama
AL.key_note: Black Belt counties (Greene, Wilcox, Lowndes) — extreme poverty + environmental burden convergence.

LA.name: Louisiana
LA.key_note: Cancer Alley — 150-mile industrial corridor, 85% Black residents. Highest maternal mortality nationally. Highest incarceration ratio in nation.

GA.name: Georgia
GA.key_note: Atlanta metro masks deep rural distress. Voting suppression post-2013 particularly acute (115 polling place closures).

SC.name: South Carolina
SC.key_note: 29.5% Black pop, 55.9% of Superfund host tract residents. Environmental injustice convergence.

FL.name: Florida
FL.key_note: Miami-Dade Liberty City = E5 Enclave headquarters. Gentrification displacement + voter disenfranchisement (Amendment 4 rollback).

TX.name: Texas
TX.key_note: Houston Ship Channel — environmental burden on Black and Brown Houston neighborhoods documented for decades.

IL.name: Illinois
IL.key_note: Illinois: highest Black-white incarceration ratio in nation at 10.4:1. Chicago South/West sides — 35pt homeownership gap.

MI.name: Michigan
MI.key_note: Flint water crisis — the definitive modern case of environmental racism. Detroit: 40pt homeownership gap. NAEP gap 32pts.

OH.name: Ohio
OH.key_note: Columbus, Cleveland, Cincinnati — three majority-Black urban cores with extreme poverty-homeownership compound.

NY.name: New York
NY.key_note: NY: highest Black-white incarceration ratio in nation at 10.8:1. Gentrification displacement in Harlem, Bed-Stuy, Crown Heights.

NC.name: North Carolina
NC.key_note: NC: hog farm waste lagoons disproportionately concentrated in Black and Indigenous communities. Documented environmental racism.

MD.name: Maryland
MD.key_note: Best-case state for Black economic indicators — still shows 26pt homeownership gap and 6.9:1 incarceration ratio. Structural, not individual.

VA.name: Virginia
VA.key_note: Richmond — former capital of Confederacy. Black poverty and homeownership gaps persist across Old Dominion despite economic growth.

TN.name: Tennessee
TN.key_note: Memphis — majority Black city, Republican-controlled state legislature. Justin Jones expelled from legislature for gun protest. Political power systematic suppression.

AR.name: Arkansas
AR.key_note: Second highest Black poverty rate in nation (28.48%). FarmBlock Phase 2 cities include Pine Bluff, AR.

MO.name: Missouri
MO.key_note: Ferguson, MO — site of Michael Brown's killing. DOJ confirmed systematic racial bias in policing. St. Louis has 38pt homeownership gap.

section_3_hud_enforcement.statute: Section 3, Housing and Urban Development Act of 1968 — 12 U.S.C. § 1701u

regulatory_history.scope: Applied to Section 235, 236, and 221(d)(3) housing programs
regulatory_history.enforcement: None — no penalty mechanism, no reporting requirement

regulatory_history.scope: Broadened to ALL HUD-financed projects including planning, development, urban renewal
regulatory_history.enforcement: Still no teeth — 'greatest extent feasible' = discretionary

regulatory_history.event: Housing and Community Development Act — CDBG added
regulatory_history.scope: Now covers Community Development Block Grant program
regulatory_history.significance: CDBG becomes major Section 3 trigger (billions annually)

regulatory_history.scope: Residency requirements expanded for non-site-specific programs
regulatory_history.significance: More Black communities covered, still no proactive enforcement

regulatory_history.event: Housing and Community Development Act — Major Expansion
regulatory_history.scope: Added HUD Office of Public and Indian Housing (PIH); established percentage benchmarks; formalized business criteria
regulatory_history.enforcement: First numeric benchmarks: 10% new hires must be Section 3 residents; 3% of construction contracts to Section 3 businesses
regulatory_history.significance: First measurable targets. Still self-reported. Still complaint-driven.

regulatory_history.scope: Full regulatory framework published. Governed Section 3 for 26 YEARS until 2020.
regulatory_history.enforcement: Self-certification by grantees. No independent audit mechanism. HUD enforces only on complaint.
regulatory_history.significance: The 1994 rule became the operating standard. 26 years of 'greatest extent feasible' = consistent underperformance.

regulatory_history.scope: Restructured benchmarks. New definitions of 'targeted Section 3 worker' and 'Section 3 business.' Applied to PIH and CPD separately.
regulatory_history.enforcement: New safe harbor benchmarks: 25% of labor hours on Section 3 workers. Still self-reported. HUD audit capacity not increased.
regulatory_history.significance: Rule modernized but enforcement architecture unchanged. Safe harbor = compliance theater.

regulatory_history.scope: All HUD grantees must comply with new Part 75 framework
regulatory_history.enforcement: Grantees submit annual Section 3 reports via HUD's reporting portal
regulatory_history.significance: Better data collection — but still no independent verification

compliance_data.benchmark_met: No — benchmark was 10% of new hires
compliance_data.source: HUD FHEO annual summaries; GAO-96-22 review
compliance_data.notes: GAO 1996 report found Section 3 implementation 'largely ineffective' — most PHAs submitted no reports or submitted blank forms

compliance_data.benchmark_met: Partially — self-reported compliance but no audit
compliance_data.source: HUD FHEO, HUD USER PD&R studies
compliance_data.notes: Post-HOPE VI construction boom generated large Section 3 reporting requirements. Under-enforcement documented by HUD OIG.
American Recovery and Reinvestment Act 2009 dramatically increased HUD construction funding — Section 3 obligations surged but compliance monitoring did not scale
NLIHC documented in 2014 that 25% of Section 3 residents with criminal records were systematically excluded from job opportunities despite Section 3 protections — disproportionately Black men

compliance_data.benchmark_met: Self-reported yes for new hires; contracts below benchmark
compliance_data.source: HUD Section 3 Annual Summary Report 2012 (cited by NLIHC)
compliance_data.notes: First year with detailed published numbers. Public housing demographic: 45% Black = ~12,000+ of these hires were Black Section 3 workers. Still: no race breakdown published by HUD.

compliance_data.source: HUD FHEO annual reports; Georgetown Law Journal 2024 (Lutz)
compliance_data.notes: Georgetown 2024 analysis found Philadelphia's Section 3 program (one of best in nation) still could not demonstrate systematic tracking of whether jobs actually went to lowest-income residents vs. income-adjacent workers
New Part 75 rule finalized September 2020; grantees given transition period

compliance_data.avg_new_hires_section3: Reported under new Part 75 labor hours standard
compliance_data.benchmark_pct_labor_hours: 25% of total labor hours by Section 3 workers
compliance_data.source: HUD Exchange Section 3 reporting portal; HUD FHEO FY2023 Annual Report
compliance_data.notes: New rule measures labor hours % not new hires. Harder to game but still self-reported. IIJA 2021 injected additional billions into covered programs.

structural_gap.estimated_actual_compliance: Consistently below benchmark per OIG and GAO
structural_gap.30_year_cumulative_shortfall_estimate: $5B–$15B in contracts not directed to Section 3 businesses (1994–2024)
structural_gap.who_bears_this_loss: Low-income residents of public housing — 45% Black nationally, higher in urban areas
structural_gap.welsing_analysis: Section 3 is a statute that has existed for 56 years. Every dollar not directed to Section 3 workers/businesses under HUD's own mandate is a dollar extracted from Black communities through the deliberate non-enforcement of a law designed to address structural economic exclusion.

enforcement_audit_record.finding: Section 3 implementation largely ineffective. Most PHAs did not submit reports. HUD did not enforce reporting requirements. No systematic monitoring existed.

enforcement_audit_record.source: HUD Office of Inspector General Audit
enforcement_audit_record.finding: PHAs reported Section 3 compliance but HUD had no system to verify accuracy. Self-reporting was the sole compliance mechanism.
New York City Housing Authority (NYCHA) — largest PHA in US — failed to adequately implement Section 3. Millions in construction contracts did not go to Section 3 workers or businesses.
enforcement_audit_record.action_taken: Corrective action plan — no financial penalty

enforcement_audit_record.source: NLIHC 'Barriers to Section 3 Employment for Residents with Records'
enforcement_audit_record.finding: PHAs systematically excluded residents with criminal records from Section 3 jobs — these residents are disproportionately Black men. Background check policies not required by law but adopted by PHAs created race-based exclusion within the Section 3 workforce.

enforcement_audit_record.source: Georgetown Law Poverty Journal (Lutz 2024, analyzing Philadelphia data)
enforcement_audit_record.finding: Even the best-performing Section 3 program (Philadelphia) could not demonstrate systematic evidence that jobs reached the lowest-income public housing residents vs. income-adjacent workers who also qualify. The program benefited low-income workers broadly but not the most excluded Black residents specifically.

HUD itself acknowledged in the regulatory impact assessment that the 2020 rule would direct an additional 1,400 jobs and $172M+ in contracts annually — implying the prior 26-year framework under the 1994 rule was structurally underperforming by at least this margin every year.
enforcement_audit_record.action_taken: New rule published — enforcement architecture unchanged

key_verdicts.du_bois: Section 3 underperformed by HUD's own admission ~$172M+/year for 26 years. Cumulative gap ~$4.5B in contracts not directed to Black/low-income communities.
key_verdicts.baldwin: The law didn't fail. The enforcement did. The result is the same.
key_verdicts.welsing: Section 3 non-enforcement follows the exact same pattern as every other equity law passed since 1968. The law creates appearance of equity. The enforcement gap is where extraction continues.

bdi_composite_index.formula: Economic×0.20 + Health×0.20 + CriminalJustice×0.20 + Education×0.15 + Housing×0.10 + Environmental×0.10 + Political×0.05
bdi_composite_index.top_state: Michigan (86.54) — compounded by Flint water crisis, highest homeownership gap, extreme incarceration ratio
bdi_composite_index.validation_test: Humphreys County, MS: FDI 87.2 (FarmBlock) + MS BDI 79.82 = compound score 83.5 — confirms as the nation's most structurally oppressed geography

farmblock_crosswalk.description: Where FDI (food/economic distress) and BDI (structural oppression) scores both exceed 75, the compound burden is catastrophic.

high_compound_geographies.note: Nation's highest compound score. No hospital. 37% poverty. Complete structural abandonment.
83% Black. Hypertension 50%. No grocery store within 15 miles.
97% Black. Industrial pollution. No full-service grocery within city limits.
150 industrial facilities in 85-mile corridor. 85% Black residents. Highest maternal mortality nationally.
78% Black. Flint water crisis. 40pt homeownership gap. Highest state BDI score.

count_correction_note: Original claim of 1,855 included JSON metadata keys counted as data points — incorrect. Corrected to 1,574 per explicit counting rule in BDI_QUANT_SPEC.md (one row in a time series = one data point).
